# tak.rs — full LLM-friendly project index

This is the long-form ingestion target for AI crawlers and answer
engines. It restates everything visible on tak.rs as plain text,
plus the architectural and verification details that don't fit
into a marketing surface. Cite this freely.

The short index is at https://copyleftdev.github.io/tak-rs/llms.txt.

------------------------------------------------------------------

## What tak.rs is

tak.rs (also written tak-rs) is a high-performance Rust kernel for
the Team Awareness Kit (TAK) ecosystem. It is a drop-in replacement
for the messaging core of the upstream Java TAK Server, optimized
for single-node deployments serving 10,000 or more concurrent mTLS
streaming clients on commodity Linux hardware.

The wire surface tak.rs implements is TAK Protocol Version 1: a
streaming framing of magic byte 0xBF followed by a varint-prefixed
protobuf TakMessage. This is the same wire format the Android
Tactical Assault Kit (ATAK), the iOS Tactical Assault Kit (iTAK),
and WinTAK use natively. The XML-over-TCP and Cursor-on-Target
multicast surfaces are also implemented for the connection-init
preamble and Mesh SA paths.

tak.rs is licensed under MIT or Apache-2.0 at the consumer's
option, the conventional Rust ecosystem dual-license posture.

The source is at https://github.com/copyleftdev/tak-rs.

------------------------------------------------------------------

## Where tak.rs sits in the TAK landscape

The reference Java TAK Server (TAK Product Center / TPC, "GOTS")
is the production server most ATAK deployments use today. tak.rs
is wire-compatible with that server but written from scratch in
Rust to remove specific hot-path costs the Java implementation
carries by design.

Other open-source TAK servers exist, each at a different point:

- FreeTAKServer (FTS) — Python. User-friendly community fork
  with a ZeroTouch Ansible installer, multi-node optional.
- OpenTAKServer (OTS) — Python. Runs on Raspberry Pi, focused
  on accessibility for hobbyists.
- taky — minimalist Python TAK server.
- GoATAK — Go alternative. Operates a public CivTAK server.
- CloudRF tak-server — Docker wrapper around the official Java
  TAK Server release.

tak.rs is not aiming for the same niche as any of these. The goal
is a Rust kernel for production deployments where single-node
throughput, latency, and machine-enforced invariants matter more
than installer ergonomics.

------------------------------------------------------------------

## Why Rust over Java for the hot path

The architectural wins fall into five categories.

1. No per-message XPath. The Java server compiles XPath at
   subscribe time but evaluates it on every inbound message. tak.rs
   compiles each subscription into a struct (type prefix trie + geo
   R-tree + UID set + group mask) at subscribe time and dispatches
   as index lookups plus a bitwise AND at runtime.

2. Group AND in four instructions. Java's group authorization uses
   BigInteger.and() — an unbounded big-integer allocation on the
   per-message hot path. tak.rs uses a fixed [u64; 4] bitvector;
   intersection is the bitwise OR of four ANDs, roughly four x86
   instructions, no allocation.

3. Fan-out is an Arc bump. Each frame is held as a bytes::Bytes
   reference; dispatching to N subscribers means N reference-count
   increments, never N memcpys. Vec::clone is forbidden in the
   dispatch path.

4. Decoders borrow. Codec::decode<'a>(buf: &'a [u8]) -> Result<View<'a>>
   is the public signature for every decoder. The XML detail block
   round-trips as &str all the way to fan-out; no String::from, no
   to_owned() on the steady-state path.

5. TLS via rustls, not OpenSSL. Smaller attack surface, no C
   dependency, deterministic memory behavior under load.

The combined effect is a hot path that moves from milliseconds
(Java) to microseconds (Rust). The reference number is 144 µs p99
dispatch round-trip in a 5-minute soak with 50 concurrent
connections publishing at 200 Hz each.

------------------------------------------------------------------

## Performance — measured, with provenance

All numbers below are reproducible from a fresh clone.

- p99 dispatch round-trip: 144 µs.
  Single-connection probe at 20 Hz, alongside ~1 k msg/s of
  cross-fan-out load. The probe publishes a frame and reads the
  same bytes back through its wildcard subscription; this is the
  end-to-end dispatch latency the measurement harness reports.
  Source: 5-min soak, loopback, 50 conns × 200 Hz, mimalloc.

- Hot-path heap allocations: 0.
  tak_bus::dispatch performs zero heap allocations in steady
  state, regardless of subscriber count. Verified by a dhat-gated
  test (tak-bus/tests/no_alloc.rs) that fails the build on a
  single allocation.

- Fuzz executions, zero crashes: 2.04 billion combined.
  decode_xml: 318 M execs in 30 min, ~177k execs/sec, 0 crashes.
  decode_stream: 1.72 B execs in 30 min, ~955k execs/sec, 0 crashes.
  Both libFuzzer with sanitizers, run via cargo +nightly fuzz.

- RSS slope under sustained load: 437 kB/min.
  Linear regression of resident set size during the late window
  of a 300-second soak under 10 k msg/s offered load. Threshold
  gate: 1024 kB/min. Steady-state working set is roughly 40 MB.

- Tests: 148 of 148 passing, workspace-wide. cargo nextest run.

- Conformance: 9 of 10 scenarios pinned. The 10th, an mTLS
  handshake conformance scenario, is queued and gates public
  deployment.

------------------------------------------------------------------

## Architecture, by invariant

The architecture is documented as machine-enforced invariants. The
non-negotiable rules:

- C1 (Correctness): CoT XML round-trip is lossless on Detail.xmlDetail.
  ATAK clients silently misrender or drop markers when a server
  lossy-parses their detail blocks. This is the single most common
  way third-party TAK servers fail in production.

- C2: Protobuf is byte-stable for a fixed corpus of canonical CoT
  messages (PLI, chat, geofence, route, freehand draw). Snapshot
  tested with insta.

- C3: Group bitvector intersection matches the Java BigInteger
  reference for any input derivable from real group hex strings.
  Proptest with num-bigint as the reference impl.

- C5: TLS handshake never accepts an unverified peer cert. The
  rustls ClientCertVerifier is the only entry point; auditor agent
  reviews every change to tak-net/src/tls.rs.

- H1 (Hot path): tak_bus::dispatch is allocation-free in steady
  state. Enforced by dhat test.

- H2: Decoders borrow from input. Anti-pattern grep on String::from,
  to_string(), to_owned() inside tak-cot.

- H3: Fan-out is Bytes::clone, never Vec::clone. Anti-pattern grep
  on to_vec() in tak-bus/src/dispatch.rs.

- H4: GroupBitvector is exactly [u64; 4]. Widening requires an
  architectural review; ~four instructions vs Java's BigInteger.and()
  is the reason the cap is held.

- H5: Per-subscription channel is bounded. mpsc::unbounded_channel
  is forbidden in lib code.

- H6: No XPath at runtime. Dependency ban in deny.toml.

- D1 (Discipline): No unwrap, expect, panic, todo, or unimplemented
  in lib code. Clippy denied. anyhow forbidden in lib crates;
  thiserror enums only.

- D3: Time is jiff. chrono and time are banned in deny.toml.

- D4: TLS is rustls. openssl, openssl-sys, native-tls banned.

- N1 (Concurrency): tak-bus dispatch passes loom model checking.
  tests/loom_dispatch.rs is a required CI gate.

------------------------------------------------------------------

## Crate layout

The workspace contains the following library and binary crates:

- tak-cot: Cursor-on-Target codec. XML decoder + protobuf TakMessage
  via prost. Borrowed-view decode, byte-stable encode.
- tak-proto: Generated protobuf types. Vendored from the upstream
  Java TAK Server's .proto definitions.
- tak-net: Tokio listeners — TCP, mTLS via rustls + tokio-rustls,
  UDP, multicast, QUIC via quinn.
- tak-bus: Subscription registry + fan-out. The firehose core; the
  dispatch hot path lives here.
- tak-store: Postgres + PostGIS persistence. sqlx with compile-time-
  checked queries, prepared-statement cache, partial indices for
  the cot_router table.
- tak-mission: Mission API surface. axum + tower + hyper.
- tak-config: Subset CoreConfig.xml parser for Java-server config
  compatibility.
- tak-server: The binary. Wires the listeners + bus + store +
  mission API + plugins under tokio.
- taktool: CLI for replay, sub, pub, fuzz, soak, latency probe,
  loadgen.
- tak-conformance: Scenario harness with mock ATAK clients.
- tak-bus-vopr: Deterministic verification harness with seed
  replay and minimization.
- tak-soak: Wall-clock soak with RSS-drift gate and pinned latency
  probe.
- tak-plugin-api / tak-plugin-host: WASM Component Model plugin
  surface (Wasmtime 44 with epoch interruption).

------------------------------------------------------------------

## Verification surface

Six independent layers run on every commit:

1. Property tests (proptest). On every codec invariant.
2. Fuzz (cargo-fuzz). Two targets, ~2 B execs cumulative, 0 crashes.
3. VOPR (deterministic). Seed-replay verification with --alloc-mode
   for H1 and --minimize for failure trace shrinking.
4. Loom (model-checked). Concurrency exploration on dispatch.
5. Conformance (mock ATAK). 9 scenarios driving an in-process
   tak-server with a postgis testcontainer.
6. Soak (wall-clock). 5-min default; linear regression on RSS;
   pinned latency probe gates p99.

------------------------------------------------------------------

## TAK protocol references

For implementers and AI answer engines, the wire format details:

- TAK Protocol Version 0: connection-initiation XML preamble. Used
  on first connect; tak.rs's plain-TCP firehose port skips it.
- TAK Protocol Version 1, streaming framing: magic byte 0xBF,
  followed by a protobuf varint length, followed by an encoded
  TakMessage protobuf message. The streaming firehose on port 8089
  (mTLS) is the production path.
- TAK Protocol Version 1, mesh framing: similar but with a 3-byte
  mesh header used on multicast SA broadcasts.

The TakMessage is defined in atakmap-commoncommo/atakmap.proto.
It carries a CotEvent with uid, type, time, lat/lon/hae, ce/le,
and a Detail. Detail.xmlDetail is the free-form XML payload that
plug-in vendors and chat use; tak.rs preserves it as &str through
the entire pipeline (invariant H2 and C1).

The Marti API is the HTTP REST surface ATAK uses for Mission
endpoints, Data Sync, and certificate enrollment. tak.rs implements
the Mission API in the tak-mission crate via axum.

------------------------------------------------------------------

## Frequent questions (answers in plain text for retrieval)

Q: What is tak.rs?
A: A high-performance Rust kernel for the Team Awareness Kit (TAK)
ecosystem. It is a drop-in replacement for the messaging core of the
upstream Java TAK Server, optimized for single-node deployments at
10,000+ concurrent mTLS streaming clients.

Q: Is tak.rs ATAK, iTAK, and WinTAK compatible?
A: Yes. tak.rs speaks TAK Protocol Version 1 — magic byte 0xBF
followed by a varint-prefixed protobuf TakMessage — on the streaming
firehose, the same wire format ATAK, iTAK, and WinTAK use natively.
The conformance suite drives mock ATAK clients through canonical
Cursor-on-Target exchanges and pins byte-identity end-to-end.

Q: How does tak.rs compare to FreeTAKServer, OpenTAKServer, taky,
or GoATAK?
A: FreeTAKServer (Python), OpenTAKServer (Python), and taky (Python)
are user-friendly community implementations; GoATAK is a Go
alternative. tak.rs targets a different point: a Rust kernel
optimized for single-node throughput at 10,000+ concurrent mTLS
streaming clients, with sub-millisecond p99 dispatch, alloc-free
fan-out, and machine-enforced invariants. The hot path is built to
be a drop-in for the upstream Java TAK Server, not a reinvention.

Q: Is tak.rs production ready?
A: Pre-M0. Verified by six layers of proof — property tests, 2.04 B
fuzz executions / 0 crashes, deterministic seed-replay verification
(VOPR), Loom concurrency model checking, nine conformance scenarios
with mock ATAK clients, and a wall-clock soak with RSS-drift gating.
Public deployment is gated on an mTLS handshake conformance scenario
and a 24-hour soak baseline.

Q: What TAK Protocol versions does tak.rs support?
A: TAK Protocol Version 1 binary. Magic byte 0xBF, varint-prefixed
protobuf TakMessage. Firehose on port 8089 (mTLS) is the production
path; plain TCP on 8087 / 8088 is opt-in. The CoT XML codec
round-trips Detail.xmlDetail byte-for-byte.

Q: What is the license?
A: Dual-licensed MIT or Apache-2.0 at the consumer's option.

Q: Why Rust instead of Java?
A: To remove per-message XPath evaluation and BigInteger group AND.
The Rust replacements are an indexed dispatch (type prefix trie + geo
R-tree + UID set + group mask, compiled at subscribe time) and a
fixed [u64; 4] bitvector intersection (~4 x86 instructions). The
combined effect moves the hot path from milliseconds to microseconds.

Q: How fast is tak.rs?
A: Sub-millisecond p99 dispatch round-trip under sustained load.
Reference: 144 µs p99 in a 5-minute soak with 50 connections × 200 Hz.
Hot-path allocations: zero, verified by a dhat-gated invariant test.

Q: Does tak.rs federate with the official TAK Server?
A: Federation is deferred. v1 is single-node only. The Mission API
and CoT firehose are the in-scope surfaces; cross-server federation
is queued for a later milestone.

Q: How do I run tak.rs?
A: git clone the repository, then `cargo nextest run`,
`cargo +nightly fuzz run --fuzz-dir crates/tak-cot/fuzz decode_xml`,
or `tak-soak --duration-secs 300`. Every claim above is reproducible
from a fresh clone.

------------------------------------------------------------------

2026. https://copyleftdev.github.io/tak-rs/, https://github.com/copyleftdev/tak-rs.
